steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence reads: "Frederick Hill, one of the nearly two thousand African American [officials] selected to public office during the decade that followed the Civil War, ______ his term as a member of the state senate in 1871." The subject is "Frederick Hill" and the blank is the main verb of the only independent clause. The main verb of an independent clause must be finite — it must show tense on its own.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — began: finite past-tense verb. "Frederick Hill began his term" is a complete, grammatical independent clause. ✓
  • Choice B — to begin: an infinitive. Non-finite; cannot serve as the main verb of an independent clause without a helper. ✗
  • Choice C — beginning: a present participle. Non-finite; creates a dangling modifier, not a main verb. ✗
  • Choice D — having begun: a past participle phrase. Non-finite; cannot anchor an independent clause. ✗
That matches Choice A — began.
